stem: A company wants to reduce the risk that its Amazon S3 buckets become publicly accessible through bucket policies or ACLs. Which approach best aligns with AWS guidance?

options.text: Enable all four S3 Block Public Access settings at the AWS account level and keep Block Public Access enabled on buckets unless a specific verified use case requires customization.
Leave account-level Block Public Access disabled and rely on manually reviewing bucket ACLs and bucket policies on a regular schedule.
Configure S3 Block Public Access at the object level so sensitive objects stay private even if the bucket allows public access.
Use bucket policies as the only control because S3 Block Public Access applies only when new buckets are created.

explanation: AWS recommends turning on all four S3 Block Public Access settings for your account, and also keeping Block Public Access enabled for buckets unless you have a specific and verified need for public access. These settings override bucket policies and ACLs that would otherwise grant public access.

distractorExplanations.b: Manual review is weaker than the preventive control AWS recommends. S3 Block Public Access is designed to centrally limit public access rather than relying only on periodic audits.
distractorExplanations.c: Amazon S3 does not support Block Public Access on a per-object basis. The settings are managed at the AWS Organizations, account, bucket, or access point level.
distractorExplanations.d: S3 Block Public Access is not limited to bucket creation time. It can be applied to existing buckets and at the account level, where it helps protect current and future buckets.
